questions.question: Explain the critical section problem and its three fundamental requirements.
keyPoints: Critical section is code accessing shared resources requiring mutual exclusion
Three requirements: Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting
Mutual Exclusion: Only one process in CS at a time
Progress: If CS empty and processes want entry, selection must be made
Bounded Waiting: Limit on entries after a process requests entry to prevent starvation

questions.question: What is a race condition? Give an example to illustrate.
keyPoints: Race condition occurs when outcome depends on timing of concurrent operations
Multiple processes access/modify shared data simultaneously
Result becomes unpredictable based on interleaving
Example: Two processes decrementing a shared counter from 1 to -1 instead of 0

questions.question: Describe the structure of a process solution to the critical section problem.
keyPoints: Process has four sections: Entry Section, Critical Section, Exit Section, Remainder Section
Entry Section: Code to request permission and wait if needed
Critical Section: Code accessing shared resources
Exit Section: Code to signal release/wake waiting processes
Remainder Section: Non-critical code executing outside CS

questions.question: Explain Peterson's solution for two processes. What are its limitations?
keyPoints: Peterson's solution is a software-based algorithm for two processes
Uses two shared variables: flag[2] and turn
flag[i] indicates whether process i wants to enter CS
turn indicates whose turn it is to enter if both want entry
Limitation: Works only for two processes, not scalable

questions.question: What is the Test-and-Set instruction? How does it help in solving the critical section problem?
keyPoints: Test-and-Set is a hardware atomic instruction
It reads a boolean variable and sets it to true in one indivisible operation
Returns the old value, allowing lock implementation
Enables implementing mutual exclusion without busy-wait loops
Process can test lock, and if free, acquire atomically

questions.question: Distinguish between busy-wait and blocking synchronization mechanisms.
keyPoints: Busy-wait (spinlock): Process continuously checks condition in a loop
Wastes CPU cycles while waiting
Blocking: Process is put to sleep by OS, awakened when resource available
No CPU waste in blocking approach
Busy-wait useful for short waits, blocking for long waits

questions.question: Explain the concept of bounded waiting with an example.
keyPoints: Bounded waiting limits how many times other processes can enter after a process requests entry
Prevents starvation/live-lock
Example: If process P0 requests entry, at most N-1 other processes can enter before P0 gets admitted
Ensures fairness in critical section access
Part of three requirements for valid CS solution

questions.question: Discuss the three requirements for a valid solution to the critical section problem. For each requirement, explain what happens when it is violated.
keyPoints: MUTUAL EXCLUSION: Only one process in CS at any time - violation leads to inconsistent data
PROGRESS: If no process in CS and some want to enter, selection must be made without delay - violation causes deadlock or indefinite postponement
BOUNDED WAITING: After request, limit on other entries before admission - violation leads to starvation
All three must be satisfied for correct synchronization
Examples: Race condition (ME violation), starvation (bounded waiting violation), deadlock (progress violation)

questions.question: Explain the Bakery algorithm for N processes. How does it achieve deadlock-free and starvation-free mutual exclusion?
keyPoints: Bakery algorithm assigns unique ticket numbers to processes wanting to enter
Process with smallest ticket number enters CS (lexicographic comparison if tie)
Ticket numbering: can use shared counter or incrementing counter
Achieves mutual exclusion through ticket comparison
Progress: New arrivals get higher numbers, cannot skip ahead
Bounded waiting: Each process eventually gets lowest ticket among waiters
Deadlock-free: No circular wait possible due to total ordering
Starvation-free: FIFO ordering based on tickets ensures fairness

questions.question: Compare software-based solutions (Peterson's algorithm) with hardware-based solutions (Test-and-Set) for the critical section problem.
keyPoints: Software solutions: Written in programming language, no special hardware needed
Hardware solutions: Use atomic CPU instructions like Test-and-Set, Swap
Peterson's: Only for two processes, works on uniprocessors
Test-and-Set: Works for any number of processes, requires atomic instruction
Software: More complex, may have more overhead due to busy-wait
Hardware: Simpler to implement, efficient for small critical sections
Both aim to satisfy mutual exclusion, progress, bounded waiting
Test-and-Set is more scalable and widely used in practice

questions.question: What is deadlock? Discuss the conditions necessary for a deadlock to occur in the context of process synchronization.
keyPoints: Deadlock is a situation where processes are blocked indefinitely waiting for each other
Four necessary conditions (Coffman conditions):
1. Mutual Exclusion: Resource can be used by only one process at a time
2. Hold and Wait: Processes hold resources while waiting for others
3. No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken away
4. Circular Wait: Circular chain of processes waiting for resources held by others
In critical section context: Can occur when multiple locks are acquired in different orders
Prevention: Break any of the four conditions
Avoidance: Use algorithms like Banker's for resource allocation
Detection: Analyze resource allocation graph for cycles
